1	---
2	name: mathlib-analogist
3	description: Read-only advisor for design decisions. Given a project declaration or proposed design, locates Mathlib's idiom for the same situation, compares the project's path to it, and reports whether the project should align or deviate (with cost analysis). Catches parallel APIs, narrow definitions, and "we couldn't build the prerequisite so we made up a stand-in" patterns before they harden.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**,analogies/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me whenever the iteration introduces a new infrastructure
10	    definition or a new "API-shape" choice the project hasn't made
11	    before. I will tell you whether Mathlib already does it the right
12	    way and what the cost of NOT aligning would be.
13	
14	  ### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)
15	
16	  Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
17	  cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:
18	
19	  - You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
20	    blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
21	    write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
22	    Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
23	  - You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
24	    signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
25	    Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.
26	  - The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is
27	    unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs
28	    unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).
29	
30	  ### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)
31	
32	  Dispatch me when:
33	
34	  - Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a "parallel API"
35	    pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-
36	    modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).
37	  - The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems
38	    wrong for downstream consumers.
39	  - **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with "design-shape
40	    suspected" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective
41	    for design-related stuck routes — bridge lemmas multiplying
42	    around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's
43	    shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.
44	
45	  ### Strict severity
46	
47	  When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose
48	  a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the
49	  project's path works. "Works" is not the bar when the cost is API
50	  fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume
51	  the unified Mathlib version.
52	
53	  I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my "PROCEED" verdict as the
54	  minimum; treat my "ALIGN WITH MATHLIB" verdict as a refactor
55	  obligation, not a suggestion.
56	
57	  I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future
58	  iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.
59	---
60	
61	# Mathlib Analogist
62	
63	You are the read-only Mathlib-analogist subagent. The plan agent points you at a project declaration, a proposed definition, or a design question, and asks "what would Mathlib do, and is the project doing that?". Your job:
64	
65	1. Identify the actual design decisions at play (often multiple compressed into one question).
66	2. Find how Mathlib has resolved analogous decisions.
67	3. Compare the project's path to Mathlib's idiom.
68	4. Report whether the project should align with the idiom — and, if not, what the cost of the divergence is.
69	
70	You do **not** rubber-stamp the project's choice. If Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project skipped it (most often because the prerequisite infrastructure looked hard), say so plainly. "It works" is not enough — the cost of API fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and code that can't compose with Mathlib is real.
71	
72	## Scope
73	
74	**One design question per invocation.** A design question may be broad ("how should we represent the Picard scheme?") or narrow ("instance-based vs. predicate-based"). Multiple Mathlib precedents in the SAME question are welcome. What you should NOT do is sprawl across unrelated decisions in one call.
75	
76	**Read-only.** You may read:
77	
78	- Project files (`.lean`, blueprint chapters, `references/`).
79	- Mathlib (via `archon-lean-lsp` MCP: `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, hover, signature lookup).
80	- Any existing `analogies/<slug>.md` summaries from prior calls.
81	
82	You may write:
83	
84	- `analogies/<slug>.md` — persistent design-rationale file future iters re-read.
85	- `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path) — your report.
86	
87	You may **NOT** modify project source, blueprint, or any state file.
88	
89	## Directive Format
90	
91	```markdown
92	# Mathlib Analogist Directive
93	
94	## Slug
95	<slug>
96	
97	## Design question
98	<the question. One question per directive.>
99	
100	## Project artifact(s) under question
101	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
102	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
103	
104	## Why now
105	<one or two sentences: what the dispatching agent is about to do (write, refactor, decide), and why a Mathlib precedent would inform it.>
106	
107	## Hints (optional)
108	<Mathlib namespaces, related concepts, or specific declarations the dispatcher suspects are relevant.>
109	
110	## Severity expectation
111	<one of: routine | high-stakes>
112	- routine: cheap sanity check on a small choice
113	- high-stakes: this design will be load-bearing; be strict about idiom adherence
114	```
115	
116	## Workflow
117	
118	1. **Read the directive completely.**
119	
120	2. **Read the project artifact(s)** and their surrounding context — imports, neighboring declarations, the blueprint chapter the declaration corresponds to (per the `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). The blueprint often makes the mathematical intent explicit where the Lean only hints at it.
121	
122	3. **Identify the open design decision(s).** A directive that says "should this be a typeclass?" usually compresses several decisions (bundled vs unbundled, predicate vs structure, instance vs explicit). Name each.
123	
124	4. **Locate Mathlib precedents.** Use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` on the relevant names and types. Read 2–5 Mathlib files that resolve analogous decisions. For each, note:
125	   - What Mathlib chose (typeclass / structure / predicate / function).
126	   - Why (legible from the docstring, naming convention, or how the choice composes with surrounding API).
127	   - How the project's current or proposed approach compares.
128	
129	5. **Compare and judge.** For each open decision, write:
130	   - The Mathlib idiom (with citation: `Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
131	   - The project's path (or proposed path).
132	   - The gap: identical, divergent-but-equivalent, divergent-with-cost, divergent-and-wrong.
133	   - For "divergent-with-cost" and "divergent-and-wrong": the concrete cost (bridge lemmas needed, downstream files that can't consume the project's API, parallel infrastructure that will eventually need to be unified).
134	
135	6. **Render a verdict per decision.** One of:
136	   - **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — the project should use the idiom; if shipped code diverges, refactor.
137	   - **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY** — divergence is the right call for this project; document why in `analogies/<slug>.md`.
138	   - **PROCEED** — no Mathlib precedent applies; project's path is reasonable.
139	   - **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** — Mathlib doesn't have the idiom yet; the project must build it (this is the case for genuinely new infrastructure).
140	
141	7. **Write the persistent analogy file** to `analogies/<slug>.md`. Format below.
142	
143	8. **Write the report** to `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md`.
144	
145	## Persistent file format (`analogies/<slug>.md`)
146	
147	```markdown
148	# Analogy: <design question>
149	
150	## Slug
151	<slug>
152	
153	## Iteration
154	<NNN>
155	
156	## Question
157	<the directive's design question, verbatim>
158	
159	## Project artifact(s)
160	- <file>:<lines> — <one-line summary>
161	- ...
162	
163	## Decisions identified
164	
165	For each open decision, one block:
166	
167	### Decision: <name>
168	
169	- **Mathlib idiom**: <precedent>. Cite: `Mathlib.X.Y.Foo` (path:line). Why Mathlib chose it: <one paragraph>.
170	- **Project's current path**: <what the project does or proposes>.
171	- **Gap**: identical | divergent-equivalent | divergent-with-cost | divergent-and-wrong.
172	- **Cost of divergence (if any)**: <bridge lemmas, fragmented API, downstream blockage>.
173	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY | PROCEED | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
174	
175	## Recommendation
176	
177	<one paragraph: what the project should do given the verdicts above. If ALIGN, what the refactor should look like.>
178	```
179	
180	## Report format
181	
182	```markdown
183	# Mathlib Analogist Report
184	
185	## Slug
186	<slug>
187	
188	## Iteration
189	<NNN>
190	
191	## Question
192	<verbatim>
193	
194	## Verdicts (summary)
195	
196	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
197	|---|---|---|
198	| <name> | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | critical |
199	| <name> | PROCEED | informational |
200	| ... | | |
201	
202	## Must-fix-this-iter
203	
204	Every ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB verdict where the project has already shipped
205	divergent code lands here automatically. Do not under-classify.
206	
207	- <decision name>: project's `<file>:<lines>` should be refactored to use Mathlib's `<idiom>`. The current parallel API causes <cost>.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Major
211	
212	ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB verdicts where the project hasn't shipped yet (still in proposal stage) — the planner can simply adopt the idiom rather than refactor.
213	
214	## Informational
215	
216	DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY (with rationale captured in `analogies/<slug>.md`) and PROCEED verdicts. NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL is also informational here — the gap is upstream, not a project failure.
217	
218	## Persistent file
219	- `analogies/<slug>.md` — design-rationale captured for future iters.
220	
221	Overall verdict: one sentence.
222	```
223	
224	## Return value
225	
226	Your final assistant message:
227	
228	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> decisions analyzed, <M> ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`
229	- Paths to the persistent file and the report.
230	
231	## Reminders
232	
233	- **Don't rubber-stamp.** When Mathlib has the idiom, say so clearly. "Works" is not enough.
234	- **Cite, don't allude.** Every Mathlib reference must be a real path + line. Use the LSP tools to verify.
235	- **Severity is strict.** ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on shipped code is must-fix-this-iter — the cost compounds with every iter the divergence persists.
236	- **Cost is concrete.** "Bridge lemmas" / "parallel API needing N translations" / "downstream files blocked" — name the specific cost, not a generic "fragmentation".
237

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no mandatory dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Analogy: Mathlib LOC cost of M3 — building a positive-genus `JacobianWitness`
2	
3	## Slug
4	m3-route-audit
5	
6	## Iteration
7	123
8	
9	## Question
10	
11	For an arbitrary smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over a
12	field `k` with `genus C ≥ 1`, what is the cumulative LOC of upstream
13	Mathlib work the project would need to contribute (or backfill in-tree)
14	in order to discharge the existential `positiveGenusWitness` —
15	equivalently, the higher-genus case of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`
16	(`AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:176`)?
17	
18	The question is asked over two prescribed construction routes (FGA via
19	Picard, and symmetric-power via Stein factorisation), with a tiebreaker
20	on cross-utility for general Mathlib AG infrastructure, and a hard
21	escalation rule at the 5000-LOC mark.
22	
23	## Project artifact(s)
24	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:143-179` — `JacobianWitness` bundled
25	  structure and `nonempty_jacobianWitness` sorry, the M3 target.
26	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:49-114` — `IsAlbanese` predicate and
27	  `IsAlbanese.unique` (positive-genus need only the existence of one
28	  Albanese witness).
29	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Genus.lean:65-68` — the project's working definition
30	  `genus C := Module.finrank k (H¹(C, O_C))`, which constrains Route B's
31	  RR formulation.
32	
33	## Mathlib snapshot used
34	
35	Mathlib package shipped with this project's lake manifest (b80f227
36	neighbourhood). Concretely:
37	
38	- `AlgebraicGeometry/` directory contents: `AffineScheme`, `AffineSpace`,
39	  `EllipticCurve/{Affine,DivisionPolynomial,Jacobian,Projective}`,
40	  `Geometrically/{Connected,Integral,Irreducible}`, `Group/{Abelian,Smooth}`,
41	  `IdealSheaf`, `Modules/{Presheaf,Sheaf,Tilde}`,

92	## Decisions identified
93	
94	Per the directive the "decision" surface is "which of the two strategy
95	routes does the project pick?", decomposed into a per-piece LOC count
96	plus tiebreakers. The structure below follows that.
97	
98	### Route A — Picard scheme via FGA
99	
100	#### Piece A1: Hilbert scheme representability for projective schemes
101	
102	- **Mathlib idiom**: `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LocalRepresentability.isRepresentable`
103	  (Stacks 01JJ) is the general gluing tool. The **specific** Hilbert /
104	  Quot representability theorems (Stacks 0DPA, 0DPB) are absent. There
105	  are no `HilbertFunctor` / `HilbertScheme` / `QuotFunctor` / `QuotScheme`
106	  declarations anywhere in `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/`.
107	- **Partial infrastructure present**:
108	  - Locally-representable-implies-representable
109	    (`...Sites.Representability:201`).
110	  - The Zariski big site and sheaf condition (`...Sites.BigZariski`).
111	  - Properness, finite type, finite presentation, flat morphisms as
112	    `MorphismProperty` (`...Morphisms.{Proper,FinitePresentation,Flat,…}`).
113	  - `ProjectiveSpectrum` of a graded ring (`...ProjectiveSpectrum/`).
114	  - Abstract finite-presentation of `SheafOfModules`
115	    (`...ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent`).
116	- **The gap proper**:
117	  1. **Quasi-coherent sheaves on a scheme**: define the typeclass
118	     `Scheme.QuasiCoherent` for a `SheafOfModules X.ringSheaf` (matching
119	     Stacks 01BD) and supply the affine/Tilde calibration; build the
120	     `qcoh(X)` abelian category. ~400-500 LOC.
121	  2. **Coherent sheaves on Noetherian schemes** + finite-presentation
122	     equivalence. ~300-400 LOC.
123	  3. **Flatness module-side**: `Module.Flat` lifted to `SheafOfModules`,
124	     and the morphism flatness `RingHom.Flat` lifted to flatness of
125	     pullback functors. ~200-300 LOC.
126	  4. **Hilbert functor**: the Yoneda-style "flat closed subschemes with
127	     fixed Hilbert polynomial" presheaf; sheaf condition for the fpqc
128	     topology (or, more cheaply, just the Zariski sheaf condition).
129	     ~400-600 LOC.
130	  5. **Grothendieck flattening stratification** (Stacks 052F, 0BFR — the
131	     hard step): every coherent sheaf becomes flat after passing to a
132	     finite locally-closed stratification of the base. This is the
133	     load-bearing technical theorem for Hilbert/Quot representability.
134	     ~1500-2200 LOC. (Stacks devotes a substantial chunk of "More on
135	     Flatness" to this; Mathlib has nothing comparable.)
136	  6. **Hilbert scheme representability for `ℙ^N`** via Grothendieck's
137	     construction (use flattening to produce relatively-flat subschemes,
138	     then glue via `LocalRepresentability`). ~600-900 LOC.
139	
140	  **Subtotal A1**: ~3400-4900 LOC.
141	
142	- **Cross-utility**: **HIGH**. Quasi-coherent + coherent + flattening +
143	  Hilbert is the foundation for every moduli-theoretic statement in
144	  algebraic geometry. The first three sub-pieces (quasi-coherent,
145	  coherent, sheaf-flatness) are unconditionally desirable in Mathlib and
146	  could plausibly be PR'd independent of the Jacobian project.
147	
148	#### Piece A2: Quot scheme representability
149	
150	- **Mathlib idiom**: same `LocalRepresentability` framework. `Quot`
151	  generalises Hilbert by replacing "closed subscheme" with "quotient
152	  sheaf of a fixed coherent sheaf". If A1's Hilbert construction is
153	  available, the additional work is:
154	  1. **Quot functor** for a fixed coherent sheaf `F` on `ℙ^N`. ~400-600 LOC.
155	  2. **Sheaf condition + flattening reuse**: leverage A1.5; the new
156	     content is bookkeeping around `F` instead of `O`. ~300-500 LOC.
157	  3. **Representability via local data + Hilbert**: ~400-600 LOC.
158	
159	  **Subtotal A2 (post-A1)**: ~1100-1700 LOC.
160	
161	  If Hilbert were *not* built first, Quot alone would still need ~80% of
162	  A1's work plus its own ~1100, i.e. ~4000+. Treat Hilbert as the
163	  natural prerequisite.
164	
165	- **Cross-utility**: **HIGH**. Quot is the universal moduli object
166	  underlying coherent-sheaf moduli, Hilbert, and Picard. Same Mathlib-PR
167	  attractiveness as A1.
168	
169	#### Piece A3: Identity-component `G^0 ⊆ G` as a closed subgroup scheme
170	
171	- **Mathlib idiom**: Mathlib has `Subgroup.connectedComponentOfOne`
172	  (`Mathlib.Topology.Algebra.Group.Basic:740`) for **topological** groups
173	  as a `Subgroup` on the underlying point set. The scheme-theoretic
174	  analog — `G^0 ⊆ G` as a *reduced closed subscheme* carrying its own
175	  group-scheme structure — is **absent**. The Picard *scheme* of `C` is
176	  in general not connected; the Jacobian is the connected component of
177	  the identity, so this piece is load-bearing for Route A even after
178	  representability of the full Picard scheme is settled.
179	- **Partial infrastructure present**:
180	  - `GrpObj` for objects of `Over (Spec (.of k))`
181	    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Cartesian.Grp_`).
182	  - Group-scheme commutativity under proper geometrically integral
183	    (`...Group.Abelian:128`).
184	  - Smoothness from `IsReduced + LocallyOfFinitePresentation`
185	    (`...Group.Smooth:38`).
186	  - Topological connected components on the underlying space of a
187	    scheme (via `TopCat`).
188	  - Reduced closed subscheme `(IdealSheafData/`) construction with
189	    associated `subschemeι`.
190	- **The gap proper**:
191	  1. **Connected components of `G.left` are open** for `G` locally of
192	     finite type over a field (uses `JacobsonSpace`, already in
193	     Mathlib). ~150-200 LOC.
194	  2. **Distinguished open + closed component containing the identity**
195	     as a reduced closed subscheme of `G`, packaged as a
196	     `Scheme.Opens`-and-reduced-closed structure. ~200-300 LOC.
197	  3. **Group-scheme structure** on `G^0`: `μ`, `inv`, `η` restrict
198	     because they're continuous and send connected components to
199	     connected components. ~300-400 LOC.
200	  4. **Geometric irreducibility / smoothness inheritance** (needs the
201	     dimension-of-component theorem and base-change closure).
202	     ~200-300 LOC.
203	
204	  **Subtotal A3**: ~850-1200 LOC.
205	
206	- **Cross-utility**: **MEDIUM-HIGH**. Identity-component-as-closed-subgroup
207	  is the entry point for the structure theory of algebraic groups
208	  (Chevalley decomposition, abelian-variety quotients). Independent of
209	  the Jacobian project, this is a clean Mathlib PR target.
210	
211	**Route A cumulative LOC**: A1 + A2 + A3 ≈
212	**~5350-7800 LOC** (using A1 = 3400-4900, A2 = 1100-1700, A3 = 850-1200).
213	
214	A point estimate: **~6500 LOC** is a reasonable midpoint.
215	
216	---
217	
218	### Route B — Symmetric powers + Stein factorisation
219	
220	#### Piece B1: Symmetric powers `Sym^n X` with smoothness
221	
222	- **Mathlib idiom**: there is **no** `Sym^n` for schemes anywhere in
223	  `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.*`. `SymmetricPower` exists in
224	  `LinearAlgebra.TensorPower.Symmetric` (the linear-algebraic
225	  construction, totally unrelated). Mathlib has no `Scheme.action`
226	  typeclass, no group-action-on-scheme infrastructure, and no scheme
227	  quotient by a finite group.
228	- **Partial infrastructure present**:
229	  - Finite products of schemes (via `CartesianMonoidalCategory` /
230	    pullbacks).
231	  - `Finset.symm` / `Equiv.Perm` and `SymmetricGroup` at the type level.
232	  - Quotients by finite group actions at the *ring* level
233	    (invariant subrings); the affine case `Spec R^G` is reachable from
234	    existing material.
235	- **The gap proper**:
236	  1. **Action of a finite group on a scheme** as a structured typeclass
237	     (`Scheme.MulAction G X` style). ~250-400 LOC.
238	  2. **Affine quotient `Spec (R^G)`** + the universal property
239	     (Stacks 03AP, 04AD). ~500-700 LOC.
240	  3. **Global quotient `X / G`** for a finite group acting on a scheme,
241	     glued from affine charts (requires the action to permute a covering
242	     family; for the `S_n` action on `X^n` this needs the symmetric
243	     stratification of `X^n`). ~800-1200 LOC.
244	  4. **Smoothness of `Sym^n C` for `C` a smooth curve**
245	     (Fogarty / Hartshorne-Mumford): the action is **not free** (the
246	     fixed locus is the big diagonal), so the quotient is not étale on
247	     `X^n`; nonetheless `Sym^n C` is smooth as a special property of
248	     1-dimensional `X`. This proof is delicate (typically via local
249	     Lüroth-style coordinates / SGA-1-style descent). ~600-1200 LOC.
250	  5. **Abel-Jacobi map `Sym^g(C) ⟶ Pic^g(C)`** in the symmetric-power
251	     model (depends on B2 + B3 for the target). ~200-300 LOC.
252	
253	  **Subtotal B1**: ~2350-3800 LOC.
254	
255	- **Cross-utility**: **LOW-MEDIUM**. Sym-powers and finite-group-quotients
256	  are useful (Chow groups, intersection theory, equivariant geometry),
257	  but the **specific result needed for Jacobians** — smoothness of
258	  `Sym^n C` for `n = g` and `C` a curve — is highly curve-specialised.
259	  The general finite-group-quotient infrastructure (B1.1–B1.3) has
260	  meaningful Mathlib utility on its own; B1.4 is narrow.
261	
262	#### Piece B2: Stein factorisation
263	
264	- **Mathlib idiom**: not present. The 0-dimensional **global** case is in
265	  `AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
266	  (`...Morphisms.Proper:154`): for integral `X / Spec K` universally
267	  closed and locally of finite type, `Γ(X, ⊤)` is finite over `K`. This
268	  gives the constant-Hom case but not the relative version.
269	- **Partial infrastructure present**:
270	  - `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (presheaf-of-modules pushforward,
271	    `...Modules.Sheaf:167`); but no coherence-preservation theorem.
272	  - `Scheme.Hom.appTop` and proper-morphism `Γ`-finiteness for the
273	    global case.
274	  - Properness, separated, finite-type, finite morphisms as
275	    `MorphismProperty`.
276	  - `Spec` as a (contravariant) functor `CommRingCat ⥤ Scheme`; but
277	    **no `RelativeSpec` / `Spec_Y` of a quasi-coherent algebra** —
278	    the construction that produces a scheme `Spec_Y A` from a
279	    quasi-coherent `O_Y`-algebra `A` is absent.
280	- **The gap proper**:
281	  1. **Coherent direct image** (Stacks 02O5 / 0205): if `f : X → Y` is
282	     proper between Noetherian schemes and `F` is coherent on `X`, then
283	     `R^i f_* F` is coherent on `Y`. This depends on Quasi-coherent +
284	     Coherent + Čech infrastructure. The 0-th case (`f_*`) suffices for
285	     Stein, and is itself sizeable. ~1200-1800 LOC just for `i = 0`.
286	  2. **Relative Spec functor**: `Spec_Y : QcohAlg(Y)^op ⥤ Sch/Y`,
287	     adjunction with global-sections-as-algebra. This is **the missing
288	     basic constructor** for Stein and several other moduli operations.
289	     ~700-1100 LOC.
290	  3. **Stein factorisation proper**: package the above as
291	     `X → Spec_Y(f_*O_X) → Y` with the connected-fibres / finite
292	     factorisation property (Stacks 03GX). ~300-500 LOC.
293	
294	  **Subtotal B2**: ~2200-3400 LOC.
295	
296	- **Cross-utility**: **HIGH**. Coherent direct image (B2.1) and relative
297	  Spec (B2.2) are foundational and would be Mathlib-welcome PRs in
298	  their own right (relative Spec is also needed by Route A: every
299	  affine map factors through `Spec_Y` of its image algebra). Stein
300	  factorisation per se (B2.3) is narrower but cheap once B2.1–B2.2 land.
301	
302	#### Piece B3: Riemann-Roch + Brill-Noether (curve side)
303	
304	- **Mathlib idiom**: not present in any usable form. The
305	  `EllipticCurve/` subtree is the Weierstrass-equation model and is
306	  **not** a general divisor / line-bundle / RR theory; it does not
307	  generalise to higher genus. There is no Cartier or Weil divisor on a
308	  general scheme, no `Pic^d`, no degree map, no Riemann-Roch, and no
309	  Brill-Noether anywhere in Mathlib.
310	- **Partial infrastructure present**:
311	  - The project's `Scheme.HModule k _ 1`
312	    (`AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean`)
313	    computes `H¹(C, O_C)` as a `ModuleCat k`. This is the definition of
314	    `genus C` and is the **anchor** for any RR statement the project
315	    can use. Any Mathlib-side RR formulation must be reconciled with
316	    this definition.
317	  - `Module.finrank`, `FiniteDimensional` in Mathlib.
318	- **The gap proper**:
319	  1. **Cartier divisors on a scheme**: invertible-section / order-of-
320	     vanishing infrastructure, with the smooth-curve specialisation
321	     where Cartier and Weil agree. ~400-600 LOC.
322	  2. **Weil divisors on a smooth curve** + equivalence and the
323	     class-group identification with `Pic(C)`. ~400-600 LOC.
324	  3. **Degree of a divisor** on a smooth proper curve over `k`
325	     (via the residue-field-degree weighted sum). ~250-350 LOC.
326	  4. **Finiteness of `dim_k H^0(C, L)` and `dim_k H^1(C, L)`** for every
327	     line bundle `L` (needs Čech cohomology for coherent sheaves on a
328	     proper curve — this leans on B2.1's coherent direct image
329	     specialised to `Y = Spec k`). ~400-700 LOC.
330	  5. **Riemann-Roch**:
331	     `h^0(D) - h^1(D) = deg D + 1 - g` (Stacks 0BSC).
332	     Either via Serre duality (more infrastructure, especially the
333	     dualising sheaf) or via the additivity / induction-on-points
334	     proof. ~500-800 LOC; +400-700 LOC if a working Serre-duality
335	     formalism (cf. `analogies/serre-duality.md`) is required as an
336	     intermediate.
337	  6. **Brill-Noether-style surjectivity / smoothness**: the AJ map
338	     `Sym^g(C) → Pic^g(C)` is birational and surjective, so its image
339	     (after Stein-factorisation B2.3) is a proper smooth variety
340	     `Pic^g(C)`. ~400-700 LOC.
341	  7. **Translation `Pic^g(C) → Pic^0(C) = Jac(C)` via a fixed point
342	     and abelian-variety structure** (group law from the symmetric-
343	     power-of-points addition). ~300-500 LOC.
344	
345	  **Subtotal B3**: ~2650-4250 LOC (low end if Serre duality is finessed,
346	  high end if a full dualising-sheaf is built; the project's existing
347	  `analogies/serre-duality.md` argues that the cheap path still costs
348	  several hundred LOC).
349	
350	- **Cross-utility**: **MEDIUM**. Cartier / Weil / Pic for curves is
351	  broadly useful. RR itself is curve-specialised but is one of the most
352	  desirable Mathlib targets in its own right. The downstream
353	  Brill-Noether content is narrow.
354	
355	**Route B cumulative LOC**: B1 + B2 + B3 ≈
356	**~7200-11450 LOC** (B1 = 2350-3800, B2 = 2200-3400, B3 = 2650-4250).
357	
358	A point estimate: **~9000 LOC** is a reasonable midpoint.

363

367	- Coherent direct image (A1.4–6 reuses A1's flattening, B2.1 reuses

372	So a project that pursues *both* routes simultaneously saves ~1000-1500

378	### Decision: route choice and escalation
379	
380	- **Mathlib idiom**: neither route is "what Mathlib does" — Mathlib does
381	  not yet construct any Picard / Jacobian / Albanese object for a
382	  general curve. The closest existing Mathlib construction is the
383	  Weierstrass-equation `EllipticCurve` for genus 1, which is not the
384	  Jacobian and does not generalise.
385	- **Project's current path**: defer the existence
386	  (`nonempty_jacobianWitness`) as a single named sorry, treating both
387	  routes as out-of-scope for the autonomous loop and routing the work
388	  to an external Mathlib PR.
389	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL on either route, with the gap
390	  unambiguously larger than the strategy's hard escalation threshold.
391	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL**, with escalation to the user
392	  for an external-PR routing decision (per STRATEGY.md's
393	  > 5000-LOC fallback rule).
394	
395	## Recommendation
396	
397	**Both routes exceed 5000 LOC of upstream Mathlib work** — Route A
398	midpoint ≈ 6500 LOC, Route B midpoint ≈ 9000 LOC. The project's
399	strategy mandates escalation in this case.
400	
401	If the user wants a route ranking despite both being over-budget,
402	**Route A is preferred** for two reasons that are independent of the
403	LOC count:
404	
405	1. **Cross-utility is higher**: Route A's three gating pieces
406	   (quasi-coherent + coherent, Hilbert / Quot representability,
407	   identity-component subgroup scheme) are all in the top tier of
408	   "Mathlib AG infrastructure that's been wanted for years". Route B's
409	   `Sym^n C` smoothness (B1.4), the RR formula (B3.5), and the
410	   Brill-Noether step (B3.6) are more curve-specialised, and `Sym^n`
411	   quotient infrastructure is narrower than Hilbert/Quot.
412	2. **Route A's gating pieces compose with the project's existing
413	   `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/*` arc**: the project already ships a
414	   ring-level Picard group (`LineBundle X := (Skeleton X.Modules)ˣ` via
415	   `CommRing.Pic` analogy; see `analogies/c1-route.md`). Route A's
416	   Picard *scheme* extends this naturally; Route B sidesteps the
417	   project's Picard arc entirely and adds a parallel Sym-power /
418	   AJ-map infrastructure.
419	
420	**Smallest upstream-PR-extractable pieces** (each well under 1500 LOC,
421	each self-contained as a Mathlib PR independent of the rest of the
422	Jacobian project):
423	
424	- **Relative Spec functor** (Route B piece B2.2, ~700-1100 LOC):
425	  `Spec_Y : QcohAlg(Y)^op ⥤ Sch/Y` with the standard adjunction.
426	  Useful for both routes (it's a strict prerequisite for Stein, and a
427	  natural intermediate for affine-map factorisation in any FGA work);
428	  Mathlib reviewers would welcome this as standalone. **Highest
429	  recommend.**
430	- **Identity-component of a `k`-group scheme as a closed subgroup
431	  scheme** (Route A piece A3, ~850-1200 LOC): generalises
432	  `Subgroup.connectedComponentOfOne` to schemes; needed for both routes
433	  (B's Pic^g → Jac translation also needs it). Standalone PR.
434	- **Quasi-coherent + Coherent typeclass for `Scheme.SheafOfModules`**
435	  (Route A piece A1.1–A1.2, ~700-900 LOC): definitional layer with
436	  affine/Tilde calibration; opens the door to *both* coherent direct
437	  image (B2.1) and Hilbert representability (A1.6).
438	
439	**Alternative routes** noted but not pursued:
440	
441	- *Route C — Chevalley-decomposition assist*: define the Jacobian as
442	  the abelian-variety quotient of the connected component of an
443	  algebraic-group envelope (the symmetric-power image quotiented by
444	  its linear part). Requires Chevalley's structure theorem for
445	  connected algebraic groups, which itself bottoms out in identity-
446	  component (A3) + linear-algebraic-group infrastructure. **Not
447	  materially cheaper.**
448	- *Route D — Étale-local linearisation*: construct the Albanese via
449	  the étale fundamental group / period map. Even larger; not
450	  algebraically self-contained in Mathlib's current state.
451	- *Route E — defer indefinitely*: keep `nonempty_jacobianWitness` as
452	  the single named gap and ship the higher-genus Jacobian as a
453	  conditional theorem in `archon-protected.yaml`. This is the
454	  **status quo** and is honest accounting. It is **not** a
455	  formalisation route, it's the absence of one.
456	
457	**Therefore the iter-124 plan agent should**:
458	
459	1. Surface this audit to the user as an external-PR routing
460	   decision.
461	2. Default to **Route A** in any user-facing summary on cross-utility
462	   grounds, **but flag explicitly** that Route A is
463	   ~6500 LOC and Route B is ~9000 LOC, both well above the 5000-LOC
464	   escalation threshold.
465	3. Optionally propose a **partial extraction PR** to Mathlib —
466	   "Relative Spec functor" is the cleanest standalone item and would
467	   accelerate either route.
468	4. If the user authorises continued in-project work, the
469	   ordering A1.1 → A1.2 → B2.2 (Relative Spec) → A3 makes both
470	   routes incrementally easier without committing to either, since
471	   each of these four pieces is needed by both routes.

- A2: −6% midpoint (within ±20%);
- A3: −5% midpoint (within ±20%);

forward-looking planning, but the order of magnitude — and crucially,
the multi-year wall-clock implication of ~6000+ LOC — is unchanged.

1. **Mainline Mathlib progress on the M3-gating sub-pieces since
   `b80f227` is essentially zero.** No HilbertScheme, no
   QuotScheme, no PicardScheme, no `Coherent` typeclass, no
   FlatteningStratification, no IdentityComponent on group schemes

   no upstream activity; this is the load-bearing risk for the
   multi-year timeline.

2. **The bulk of the −430 LOC refresh delta is from iter-123
   undercount, not Mathlib motion.** Iter-123 missed the
   `IsQuasicoherent` / `IsFinitePresentation` typeclasses already
   present in `b80f227` at
   `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent:249,262`,

   on `Scheme` at `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian:57,278`.

   LOC of delta comes from the `precoverage @IsOpenImmersion`
   abstraction modernisation (A1.6, A2.3) and the
   `AlgClosed/Basic.lean` K-point identification helpers (A3.4).

3. **`Scheme.QuasiCoherent X` specialisation is the cheapest A1
   entry point.** With the bundled `IsQuasicoherent` typeclass on
   `SheafOfModules R` already present, the scheme-side wrapper
   (~80–120 LOC) plus the affine-Tilde calibration (~150–200 LOC)
   together total ~230–320 LOC of mostly definitional plumbing.
   This is the lowest-LOC starting point for any Route A in-tree
   work and is recommended as the first A1 lane the planner
   schedules.

4. **The directive's hypothetical "Hilbert representability has
   landed at `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Hilbert.Representability`"
   is FALSE.** No such file exists in either pin or mainline; no
   `Hilbert*` file exists under `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/`.
   STRATEGY.md L627 should NOT cite that path as landed.

5. **Sunk-cost-trap guardrail (STRATEGY.md L627) is honored.** The
   iter-145 refresh was dispatched in iter-145 (well before the
   iter-150 deadline). The refreshed Route A ~6070 LOC midpoint
   does not falsify the iter-144 commitment; the route remains
   ~6× the historical 5000-LOC threshold and multi-year. No
   pivot is triggered by this refresh.

## Recommendation

The iter-144 Route A commitment is preserved. The planner should:

1. **Use the iter-145 refreshed numbers** in any forward-looking LOC
   accounting (A1 ~3775 / A2 ~1320 / A3 ~975 / total ~6070 LOC at
   midpoint), not the iter-123 numbers.

2. **Continue with the iter-138 in-tree-no-PR-lane disposition**
   (STRATEGY.md L639–656). The refreshed numbers do not change the
   in-tree-vs-PR-extraction choice.

3. **Order A1 sub-pieces by entry-cost when scheduling**: `Scheme`-side
   `IsQuasicoherent` specialisation (~230–320 LOC, A1.1) → coherent
   typeclass + Stacks 01XZ equivalence (~150–250 LOC, A1.2) →

   sheaf-side flatness (~200–300 LOC, A1.3) → Hilbert functor (~400–600
   LOC, A1.4). Defer A1.5 flattening (~1500–2200 LOC) — the largest
   single item — until the prerequisites land, since its proof depends
   on the coherent / Noetherian / flat infrastructure above.

4. **Set a re-refresh tripwire**. Re-dispatch this audit at iter-170
   (≈ 25 iters out, matching the iter-123→iter-145 cadence), OR
   earlier if any of HilbertScheme / QuotScheme / Coherent /
   FlatteningStratification / IdentityComponent lands in mainline.
   The simplest tripwire is a one-line `grep` of mainline
   `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/` for `Hilbert`, `Quot`, `Coherent`,

5. **Do not** treat the −430 LOC midpoint delta as "Route A is cheaper
   now" — it is "iter-123 was conservative by ~7% even at the time of
   writing". The iter-145 numbers are a tighter baseline, not an
   easier target.
